threats.title: SGX Instance Self-Replacement Bypasses Key Rotation and Expiry
threats.hypothesis_description: SgxVerifier._replaceInstance does not check if newInstance == oldInstance. An SGX enclave can submit proofs where it replaces itself with the same address, resetting validSince to block.timestamp. This bypasses the 180-day INSTANCE_EXPIRY and the side-channel protection requiring key rotation, allowing a single compromised SGX key to remain valid indefinitely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The INSTANCE_EXPIRY (180 days) and key rotation are critical security mechanisms for SGX-based proving. If an enclave's private key is leaked via side-channel attack, the protocol relies on key rotation to limit exposure. This bug completely negates that protection.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _replaceInstance sets instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, block.timestamp) without checking newInstance != oldInstance

preconditions: SGX instance is registered and valid
SGX enclave controls proof data construction (chooses newInstance address)
No external check prevents newInstance == oldInstance

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, uint64(block.timestamp)) with no equality check
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Enclave sets newInstance to its own address in proof data, signature is valid (self-signed), _isInstanceValid passes
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Compromised SGX key can be used to submit unlimited invalid proofs that pass verification, enabling theft of validity bonds or acceptance of fraudulent state transitions

economic_questions: What is the total value at risk from invalid SGX proofs being accepted?
How many blocks use SGX-tier proofs currently?
What is the cost of a side-channel attack on SGX enclaves?
validation_requirements: Confirm that proof.data can encode newInstance == oldInstance
Verify ECDSA.recover returns oldInstance when oldInstance signs hash including itself as newInstance
Test that _isInstanceValid passes after self-replacement

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of SGX side-channel attack (hardware + time)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for proof submission (~0.01 ETH per block)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to full L2 state manipulation enabling bridge theft

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after SGX key is compromised
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could compromise entire L2 state integrity

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All L2 users and bridged asset holders
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full loss of bridged assets if invalid state root finalized

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: INSTANCE_EXPIRY=180 days, key rotation per proof by design
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify self-replacement path in unit test

threats.title: SGX Duplicate Instance Registration via Missing addressRegistered Update
threats.hypothesis_description: When SgxVerifier._replaceInstance rotates an SGX key, it does NOT set addressRegistered[newInstance] = true. This means the newInstance address can later call registerInstance() with a valid attestation and obtain a second instance ID, bypassing the one-address-per-instance invariant.
threats.investigation_rationale: The addressRegistered mapping exists specifically to prevent one SGX enclave from having multiple valid instance IDs. If bypassed, an enclave could maintain perpetually valid instances by cycling between IDs, defeating expiry checks.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _replaceInstance writes new Instance but never sets addressRegistered[newInstance] = true

preconditions: SGX instance has been replaced at least once via verifyProof
Automata DCAP attestation is available (automata_dcap_attestation resolved to non-zero)
Enclave can produce a valid attestation for the newInstance address

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After replacement: registerInstance(_attestation) where _attestation.localEnclaveReport.reportData contains newInstance address → passes addressRegistered check → creates duplicate ID
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Enclave maintains two valid instance IDs, can bypass expiry by alternating between them

economic_questions: Can an enclave produce valid attestations for the same address post-replacement?
What is the economic value of perpetually valid SGX instances?
validation_requirements: Trace _replaceInstance to confirm addressRegistered is never set for newInstance
Confirm registerInstance succeeds for an address that was set via _replaceInstance
Verify the duplicate ID allows bypassing the 180-day expiry

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal - only gas costs for registerInstance

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After any proof submission that triggers _replaceInstance

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Unit test: verifyProof → registerInstance for same address

threats.title: Guardian Approval Hash Excludes Proof Data Enabling Unauthorized Liveness Bond Return
threats.hypothesis_description: GuardianProver.approve() computes the approval hash as keccak256(abi.encode(_meta, _tran)), excluding _proof entirely. The last guardian to trigger the threshold determines the actual proof.data passed to TaikoL1.proveBlock. A malicious guardian can include RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND (bytes32) in proof.data to trigger liveness bond return to the assigned prover, even though other guardians did not approve this action.
threats.investigation_rationale: RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND causes transfer of livenessBond (250 TKO) to the assigned prover AND sets blk.livenessBond = 0. If the malicious guardian colludes with the assigned prover, this extracts 250 TKO per block that should have been slashed or retained.

proof_of_signal.pattern: hash = keccak256(abi.encode(_meta, _tran)) - _proof is not included in approval hash

preconditions: At least one guardian is malicious or compromised
Malicious guardian is the one who triggers the minGuardians threshold (submits last)
Block's assigned prover would not normally deserve liveness bond return

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Malicious guardian crafts _proof.data = keccak256('RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND') in their approve() call, other guardians approved different proof.data
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: 250 TKO extracted per block via colluding with assigned prover: 250 TKO * N blocks

economic_questions: How many blocks could be affected?
What is the value of 250 TKO per block extraction?
Could the malicious guardian systematically target blocks where livenessBond should be slashed?
validation_requirements: Confirm proof.data is not part of guardian approval hash
Test that the last guardian's proof.data is what gets forwarded to TaikoL1
Verify RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND path triggers with guardian proof

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: 100% profit (no capital at risk for guardian)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any block where assigned prover failed to prove (should lose livenessBond)

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: 250 TKO per affected block * total blocks

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace guardian approval flow to confirm proof.data independence

threats.title: Tier Selection Gaming via Predictable On-Chain Randomness
threats.hypothesis_description: Block minTier is derived from keccak256(block.prevrandao, numBlocks, block.number), which is fully deterministic once the L1 block is known. Proposers can compute the required tier off-chain before submitting. On mainnet, 0.1% of blocks require expensive SGX_ZKVM tier. Proposers can simply skip L1 blocks where the randomness would trigger SGX_ZKVM, waiting for a block where only SGX is needed, systematically avoiding the higher security audit.
threats.investigation_rationale: The multi-tier proving system relies on random selection to ensure a percentage of blocks get higher-security proofs. If proposers can game this, the protocol's security guarantee is degraded from '0.1% of blocks verified by ZKVM' to effectively 0%.
preconditions: Proposer has off-chain computation ability (trivial)
Proposer can choose which L1 block to submit in
No penalty for delaying proposals by 1-2 blocks

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: getMinTier: if (_rand % 1000 == 0) return TIER_SGX_ZKVM; else return TIER_SGX
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Proposer computes difficulty for each L1 block, only proposes when difficulty % 1000 != 0
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Proposer saves SGX_ZKVM proving costs (validityBond difference 500-250=250 TKO per avoided block) and avoids more expensive proof generation

economic_questions: What is the cost differential between SGX and SGX_ZKVM proofs?
How many proposers actively game this selection?
What is the security cost of never having ZKVM proofs?
validation_requirements: Simulate tier selection for sequential L1 blocks
Verify proposers can compute difficulty before committing
Analyze historical block proposals for tier selection patterns

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 250+ TKO saved per avoided ZKVM block

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compute difficulty for multiple sequential block proposals

threats.title: Assigned Prover Loses Liveness Bond Despite Proving Correctly When Contested
threats.hypothesis_description: When an assigned prover correctly proves a block but is contested, a higher-tier prover must resolve the dispute. The higher-tier prover becomes ts.prover, permanently locking out the assigned prover (L1_ASSIGNED_PROVER_NOT_ALLOWED). During verification, since ts.prover != assignedProver, the assigned prover loses livenessBond/2 (125 TKO) despite their original proof being correct.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates a profitable griefing vector: any actor can force the assigned prover to lose 125 TKO by contesting their valid proof for 500 TKO. While the contester loses 375 net TKO, the damage ratio (contester:victim) of 3:1 may be acceptable for targeted attacks against competing provers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (ts.prover != blk.assignedProver) { bondToReturn -= blk.livenessBond >> 1; } — penalizes assigned prover even when their original proof was correct

preconditions: Assigned prover has submitted a valid proof at the initial tier
Attacker has contestBond (500 TKO for SGX tier) to contest
A third-party higher-tier prover resolves the dispute confirming original was correct

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: bondToReturn = validityBond + livenessBond; if (ts.prover != assignedProver) bondToReturn -= livenessBond >> 1
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Contest valid proof → higher-tier resolves → ts.prover changes to higher-tier prover → assigned prover loses livenessBond/2
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker spends 500 TKO (loses ~375 net), assigned prover loses 125 TKO. Ratio 3:1 cost-to-damage for targeted griefing.

economic_questions: Is the 3:1 griefing ratio sufficient deterrent?
Can an attacker systematically target specific provers to force them out?
What is the cumulative impact across many contested blocks?
validation_requirements: Calculate full TKO flow for contest → resolution → verification
Verify assigned prover cannot re-prove after being overridden
Test economic impact on assigned prover's long-term profitability

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Not profitable for attacker; 125 TKO damage to victim per block
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Negative for attacker (-375 TKO per block); griefing ratio 3:1

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after assigned prover submits proof
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could drive honest provers out of the market

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative: 125 TKO * number of contested blocks

threats.title: Proving Window Manipulation via Repeated Pause/Unpause Toggling
threats.hypothesis_description: The proving window for the first transition is calculated as max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) + provingWindow*60. A malicious chain_pauser can repeatedly toggle pauseProving(true)/pauseProving(false), resetting lastUnpausedAt each time. This indefinitely extends the assigned prover's exclusive proving window, preventing any other prover from submitting proofs for first transitions, effectively stalling block verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the assigned prover colludes with chain_pauser, they can maintain an exclusive proving window indefinitely without actually proving, preventing block verification and L2 finality.

proof_of_signal.pattern: inProvingWindow = uint256(_ts.timestamp).max(_state.slotB.lastUnpausedAt) + _tier.provingWindow * 60 >= block.timestamp — lastUnpausedAt resets on each unpause

preconditions: chain_pauser role is compromised or colluding
Assigned prover does not submit proofs
chain_pauser can call pauseProving frequently

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: lastUnpausedAt = block.timestamp on every unpause; max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) resets the proving window base
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Toggle pause every hour: each toggle resets window, assigned prover stays exclusive for tid==1 && tier==0
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Block verification halted indefinitely; L2 finality frozen

economic_questions: What is the economic impact of stalled L2 finality?
Can bridge withdrawals be delayed or blocked?
What is the cost of maintaining the pause toggle?
validation_requirements: Simulate pause/unpause cycle and verify proving window never expires
Confirm other provers cannot submit for tid==1 during extended window
Assess recovery mechanisms

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect - enables extortion by blocking finality
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Indefinite while chain_pauser key is available

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: SGX provingWindow=60 minutes, SGX_ZKVM provingWindow=240 minutes

threats.title: Guardian Proofs Blocked During Proving Pause Prevents Emergency Resolution
threats.hypothesis_description: TaikoL1.proveBlock has both whenNotPaused and whenProvingNotPaused modifiers. When proving is paused, guardians (the highest-authority provers) cannot submit corrective proofs. This creates a deadlock: if proving is paused to prevent damage from invalid proofs, the guardian mechanism designed for emergency override is also blocked.
threats.investigation_rationale: Guardians are the top-tier emergency override mechanism. If they cannot prove during a pause, there is no mechanism to correct disputed transitions until proving is unpaused, potentially allowing stale invalid proofs to persist.

proof_of_signal.pattern: proveBlock has whenProvingNotPaused modifier — no exemption for guardian tier proofs

preconditions: Proving is paused (provingPaused == true)
An invalid or disputed transition exists that needs guardian resolution
Guardian set is functional and willing to prove

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: whenProvingNotPaused modifier applied uniformly to all proof tiers
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Pause proving → guardians blocked → invalid transitions cannot be corrected → L2 finality frozen
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker who paused proving can hold the protocol hostage; combined with ECON-H006 creates complete deadlock

economic_questions: What is the recovery path if proving is paused and invalid proofs exist?
Can the owner unpause quickly enough to prevent damage?
What is the window of vulnerability?
validation_requirements: Confirm guardian proveBlock call reverts when provingPaused is true
Test the interaction with pause/unpause and guardian approval flow
Verify no alternative guardian proving path exists

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When an invalid proof has been submitted and needs correction

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Full protocol value during pause window

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Guardian tier cooldownWindow=60 min, provingWindow=2880 min
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate guardian proof submission during proving pause

threats.title: Liveness Bond Bypass via Custom Malicious Hook Circumventing AssignmentHook
threats.hypothesis_description: LibProposing.proposeBlock only checks that TKO balance increased by livenessBond after hooks execute. It does NOT verify that the livenessBond came from the assignedProver's approval. A proposer can deploy a custom hook that transfers livenessBond TKO from any source (including the proposer's own balance), effectively proposing blocks with an assigned prover who never consented or staked.
threats.investigation_rationale: The integrity of the proving market depends on assigned provers having skin-in-the-game via their staked livenessBond. If proposers can bypass AssignmentHook and stake on behalf of arbitrary addresses, the economic incentive alignment breaks.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (tko.balanceOf(address(this)) != tkoBalance + _config.livenessBond) revert — only checks balance delta, not source

preconditions: Proposer can deploy an arbitrary contract at an address > previous hook
Proposer has livenessBond (250 TKO) to deposit from own funds
No on-chain enforcement that hooks must be whitelisted

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deploy hook that calls tko.transfer(TaikoL1, 250e18) from proposer's TKO balance; set assignedProver to any address
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Proposer can frame any address as assignedProver. If that address doesn't prove, they lose reputation but no actual funds. Proposer's livenessBond may be returned if the framed address does prove.

economic_questions: Can this be used to grief specific provers by using their address without consent?
Does the assignedProver lockout mechanism create additional harm?
What happens to livenessBond when a non-consenting prover is named?
validation_requirements: Deploy custom hook that transfers TKO to TaikoL1 from proposer
Verify proposeBlock succeeds with non-consenting assignedProver
Test full lifecycle: propose → prove → verify with framed prover

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; enables griefing and market manipulation

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reputational harm; exclusion from proving their own blocks

threats.title: Timelock Admin Role Enables Instant Execution Bypassing Governance Delay
threats.hypothesis_description: TaikoTimelockController.getMinDelay() returns 0 for holders of TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE. Deployment script grants this role to securityCouncil. If the securityCouncil key is compromised, an attacker can instantly execute any timelock operation (including contract upgrades, address manager changes, and token burns) without the 7-day delay.
threats.investigation_rationale: The timelock's purpose is to give users time to exit before potentially malicious changes take effect. Returning 0 delay for admin completely negates this safety mechanism. AddressManager ownership is transferred to timelock, so instant execution could replace critical protocol addresses.
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol: _timelock.grantRole(_timelock.TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE(), securityCouncil); TaikoTimelockController.sol:getMinDelay returns 0 for TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE
preconditions: TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE holder (securityCouncil) key is compromised
securityCouncil also has PROPOSER_ROLE and EXECUTOR_ROLE per deployment script

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: getMinDelay() returns 0 for admin, bypassing the 7-day delay
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: schedule() + execute() in same block with delay=0: replace bridge, signal_service, or taiko_token addresses
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Complete protocol takeover: redirect bridge to attacker contract, drain all bridged assets

economic_questions: What total value is controlled by contracts under timelock authority?
Can the securityCouncil key compromise scenario be mitigated?
Is the 0-delay for admin intentional design or an oversight?
validation_requirements: Confirm securityCouncil has TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE in deployment
Verify schedule+execute with 0 delay works for critical operations
Test address replacement attack via AddressManager

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None for exploit; large for extraction

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol users and bridge depositors

threats.title: Systematic Contestation Griefing to Delay L2 Finality
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can systematically contest every valid proof at the lowest tier, forcing resolution at higher tiers. Each contest delays verification by requiring a higher-tier proof + cooldown window. On mainnet: SGX contest (500 TKO) → needs SGX_ZKVM resolution (24h cooldown). SGX_ZKVM contest (1000 TKO) → needs Guardian resolution (1h cooldown). Total cost per block: 1500 TKO. Total delay per block: ~25+ hours.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contestation mechanism allows any party to delay block verification for ~25 hours per block for 1500 TKO. At scale, this can prevent L2 finality indefinitely, blocking bridge withdrawals and cross-chain operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Contest path only requires posting contestBond and different blockHash/stateRoot; no proof verification needed at same tier

preconditions: Attacker has sufficient TKO to post contest bonds (500-1000 TKO per block)
Verifier skips proof verification when isContesting is true (SgxVerifier returns early)
Higher-tier provers exist to eventually resolve

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: SGX contestBond=500 TKO, SGX_ZKVM contestBond=1000 TKO; contester loses full bond when proven wrong (minus 25% to winner)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Contest every block at SGX tier (500 TKO each); if needed, contest SGX_ZKVM resolution (1000 TKO); each adds 24h+ delay
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not directly profitable; griefing cost is 1500 TKO/block. For 864,000 blocks (max buffer), cost would be 1.296B TKO.

economic_questions: What is the cost per hour of finality delay?
Is the contestation bond sufficient deterrent at current TKO prices?
Can automated systems escalate proofs fast enough to minimize delay?
validation_requirements: Calculate total delay from double-contestation (SGX + SGX_ZKVM)
Verify SgxVerifier.verifyProof returns early on contesting without proof check
Model optimal attacker strategy for maximum delay per TKO spent

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Delayed L2 finality reduces confidence and bridge usability

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All L2 users awaiting finality for bridge withdrawals
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All pending bridge operations during delay period

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: SGX cooldown=1440 min, SGX_ZKVM cooldown=1440 min, Guardian cooldown=60 min
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model full contest → resolution → verification timeline
